title: Structure-preserving discretization and fingering dynamics of a Cahn-Hilliard model for traction-driven digit morphogenesis
summary: We study a Cahn-Hilliard equation with anisotropic traction flux arising as a reduced continuum model of mechanically biased cell interactions in digit-forming organoids. For a regularized problem with strictly positive bounded mobility, we introduce a mixed finite element discretization based on an implicit-explicit treatment of the chemical potential. We prove existence of discrete solutions, establish exact mass conservation and a discrete energy inequality, and show convergence of the fully discrete approximations to a weak solution of the regularized problem. Numerical experiments illustrate the resulting dynamics and show the transition from classical coarsening to traction-induced fingering and protrusive growth. The computational study is complemented by mass and energy diagnostics, an energy-balance residual, fingering-onset and protrusion-count diagnostics, and a manufactured-solution convergence study.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a mixed finite element method for a Cahn-Hilliard equation with anisotropic traction flux modeling mechanically biased cell interactions in digit-forming organoids, proving theoretical properties (existence, mass conservation, energy inequality, convergence) and demonstrating fingering/protrusive growth dynamics numerically.
AI.motivation: To model and simulate mechanically biased cell interactions in digit-forming organoids (e.g., limb development), specifically the transition from classical coarsening to traction-induced fingering and protrusive growth.
AI.method: A mixed finite element discretization with implicit-explicit treatment of chemical potential for a regularized Cahn-Hilliard equation with strictly positive bounded mobility.
AI.result: Proved existence of discrete solutions, exact mass conservation, discrete energy inequality, and convergence to weak solutions. Numerical experiments show transition from coarsening to fingering/protrusive growth with supporting diagnostics.
AI.conclusion: The proposed numerical scheme is theoretically sound and capable of capturing biologically relevant traction-induced pattern formation dynamics beyond classical coarsening.

title: Cascading Smoothers for Multigrid
summary: Multigrid methods are among the most effective frameworks for solving large-scale sparse systems. However, achieving their hallmark linear scaling and rapid convergence crucially depends on an effective smoother algorithm, whose design is often highly problem-dependent. This paper develops a new approach, referred to as \textit{cascading smoothers} due to their operation as an ordered sequence of single-step block-diagonal smoothers. Each level in the cascade is optimised to maximally damp the output of prior steps via a Frobenius norm minimisation of the corresponding error propagators. In particular, we develop an additive (resp., multiplicative) formulation analogous to Jacobi (resp., Gauss-Seidel). Applied within a standard multigrid V-cycle, we show they are remarkably effective across a wide array of problems, including finite difference, finite element, and discontinuous Galerkin discretisations applied to Poisson, elliptic interface, and Stokes systems as well as multiphase variants. In every case, cascading smoothers closely match or significantly outperform their optimally-damped classical counterparts, yet require no parameter tuning apart from a few discrete solver choices. Additionally, the approach is highly parallelisable and robust to geometric and operator complexities such as unstructured meshes and high-contrast coefficients.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces "cascading smoothers," a new parameter-free multigrid smoothing approach that operates as an ordered sequence of single-step block-diagonal smoothers, optimized to maximally damp errors from prior steps via Frobenius norm minimization, and demonstrates its effectiveness across diverse discretizations (finite difference, finite element, DG) and problems (Poisson, Stokes, elliptic interfaces, multiphase flows) without requiring parameter tuning.
AI.motivation: Multigrid methods are highly effective but their convergence and linear scaling critically depend on problem-specific smoother design, which is often challenging and requires extensive tuning. The authors aim to develop a parameter-free, robust, and parallelizable smoother that works effectively across a wide range of problems without manual tuning.
AI.method: The paper develops cascading smoothers—an ordered sequence of single-step block-diagonal smoothers. Each level in the cascade is optimized to maximally damp the output of prior steps by minimizing the Frobenius norm of corresponding error propagators. Both additive (Jacobi-like) and multiplicative (Gauss-Seidel-like) formulations are developed. The smoothers are applied within a standard multigrid V-cycle.
AI.result: Cascading smoothers closely match or significantly outperform optimally-damped classical smoothers across finite difference, finite element, and discontinuous Galerkin discretizations for Poisson, elliptic interface, Stokes, and multiphase problems, requiring no parameter tuning beyond a few discrete solver choices. The method is highly parallelizable and robust to unstructured meshes and high-contrast coefficients.
AI.conclusion: Cascading smoothers provide a robust, parameter-free, and parallelizable alternative to classical smoothers in multigrid methods, achieving linear scaling and rapid convergence consistently across diverse problem types without manual tuning.

title: Hyperbolicity-Preserving Stochastic Galerkin Methods for Conservation Laws Based on Associative Truncated Products on Polynomial Spaces
summary: Stochastic Galerkin discretizations of nonlinear hyperbolic conservation laws may lose hyperbolicity because the standard pseudospectral product is generally nonassociative, leading to non-commuting blocks in the flux Jacobian matrix. We develop a novel framework for constructing hyperbolicity-preserving stochastic Galerkin systems based on associative truncated products on polynomial spaces. In one stochastic dimension, we characterize associative truncated products through a single polynomial datum and identify examples with useful symmetry, positivity, and spectral properties, including collocation products and an associative symmetric product based on Gaussian quadrature nodes. We prove a consistency result showing that, under suitable projection-error assumptions, these products converge to the classical product as the polynomial degree grows. For systems with rational fluxes, we derive sufficient conditions under which the resulting stochastic Galerkin flux remains hyperbolic on the corresponding admissible set. Applications to the one-dimensional isothermal and compressible Euler equations show accurate statistical approximation and robust hyperbolicity preservation of the computed stochastic Galerkin states.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a framework for constructing hyperbolicity-preserving stochastic Galerkin discretizations of nonlinear hyperbolic conservation laws by using associative truncated products on polynomial spaces, proving consistency and demonstrating robust performance on the Euler equations.
AI.motivation: Standard stochastic Galerkin discretizations of nonlinear hyperbolic conservation laws can lose hyperbolicity because the pseudospectral product is nonassociative, leading to non-commuting flux Jacobian blocks and numerical instability.
AI.method: The authors characterize associative truncated products via a single polynomial datum, identify examples (collocation and a symmetric product based on Gaussian quadrature nodes), prove consistency under projection-error assumptions, and derive sufficient conditions for hyperbolicity preservation in systems with rational fluxes.
AI.result: The proposed associative products preserve hyperbolicity in stochastic Galerkin systems. Applications to 1D isothermal and compressible Euler equations demonstrate accurate statistical approximation and robust hyperbolicity preservation.
AI.conclusion: Associative truncated products offer a principled way to maintain hyperbolicity in stochastic Galerkin methods, with strong theoretical guarantees and practical effectiveness for hyperbolic conservation laws.

title: On the completeness of generalized hierarchical spline spaces
summary: We introduce a general theoretical approach to hierarchical spline spaces that replaces the classical constructive definition - based on basis selection - with a descriptive formulation in terms of regularity constraints. Specifically, we define generalized hierarchical spline spaces on multi-level domains as collections of piecewise functions satisfying hierarchical contact conditions across interfaces between refinement levels. The proposed framework applies to a broad class of local function spaces and relies on a minimal abstract requirement, the extension assumption, rather than on specific polynomial properties. Within this framework, we identify rules under which the hierarchical selection mechanism yields a complete basis, in the sense that it spans exactly the space characterized by the contact conditions. As an application, we consider Tchebycheffian spline spaces. We show that spaces generated by extended complete Tchebycheff (ECT) systems fit in this framework, thereby establishing the completeness of hierarchical Tchebycheffian splines. This demonstrates that the proposed theory naturally extends beyond the polynomial setting and provides a unified foundation for hierarchical constructions in more general spline spaces.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a descriptive theoretical framework for hierarchical spline spaces based on regularity constraints rather than basis selection, proves completeness conditions, and demonstrates applicability to non-polynomial settings like Tchebycheffian splines.
AI.motivation: To replace the classical constructive definition of hierarchical spline spaces (based on basis selection) with a more general descriptive formulation using regularity constraints, enabling a unified theory that extends beyond polynomial settings.
AI.method: Define generalized hierarchical spline spaces via hierarchical contact conditions across multi-level domain interfaces, using the extension assumption (a minimal abstract requirement) instead of specific polynomial properties. Identify selection rules for complete basis spanning the space characterized by contact conditions.
AI.result: The framework applies to broad local function classes; ECT systems fit the framework; hierarchical Tchebycheffian splines are shown to be complete; the theory extends naturally beyond polynomial settings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed theory provides a unified foundation for hierarchical constructions in general spline spaces, validated through application to Tchebycheffian splines.

title: Feature-preserving Latent-EnKF for Data Assimilation of Flows with Shocks
summary: The ensemble Kalman filter (EnKF) is widely adopted for sequential data assimilation, but fails for solutions with discontinuities, such as shocks in compressible flows. Uncertainty in shock location induces multimodal ensemble statistics that violate the Gaussian assumptions underlying the EnKF, producing large-scale spurious oscillations in the analysis state. We introduce a feature-preserving latent-EnKF that performs the ensemble update in a learned low-dimensional latent space, where shock and flow features admit a smooth manifold representation, thereby preserving sharp features during EnKF analysis. The updated latent state is mapped back to physical state through a shared decoder for all ensemble members. The algorithm eliminates the member-specific ordered training and positivity flooring used in prior approaches. Numerical experiments on a Sod shock tube and Mach 2 shock interaction with a 2D cylinder, using sparse and noisy observations, show accurate feature recovery of shocks and contact discontinuities without spurious oscillations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a feature-preserving latent-EnKF that performs ensemble updates in a learned low-dimensional latent space to handle shocks and discontinuities in data assimilation, avoiding spurious oscillations that plague standard EnKF.
AI.motivation: Standard EnKF fails for compressible flows with discontinuities (shocks) because uncertainty in shock location creates multimodal ensemble statistics that violate Gaussian assumptions, producing spurious oscillations.
AI.method: A latent-EnKF that updates ensembles in a learned low-dimensional latent space where shocks admit smooth manifold representations, then maps back to physical space via a shared decoder, eliminating member-specific ordered training and positivity flooring.
AI.result: Numerical experiments on Sod shock tube and Mach 2 shock interacting with a 2D cylinder, using sparse/noisy observations, show accurate recovery of shocks and contact discontinuities without spurious oscillations.
AI.conclusion: The latent-space approach effectively preserves sharp features during EnKF analysis for problems with discontinuities, overcoming the Gaussian limitations of standard EnKF.

title: Compatibility of Higher-Order Slow-Manifold Reduction and Continuum Limits in Adaptive Networks
summary: Adaptive networks couple the evolution of node states to the evolution of the interactions between them. In fast-adapting phase oscillator networks, a slow-manifold reduction of a pairwise microscopic model can generate effective higher-order terms in the phase dynamics. We ask whether this higher-order structure survives the dense-graph continuum limit, and whether it matters if one first reduces and then passes to the continuum, or first passes to the continuum and then reduces. We prove well-posedness and discrete-to-continuum convergence for the unreduced and first-order reduced models, and we construct the continuum slow manifold directly in a Banach-space setting. Along admissible equal-cell step approximations, the two routes give the same first-order continuum vector field, including the same pairwise correction and triplet operator, up to controlled $O(\varepsilon^2)$ remainders. A continuum mixed-derivative criterion then shows that, for suitable coupling functions, the resulting triplet operator is genuinely nonpairwise in the smooth bounded-kernel class. Thus the higher-order term is not a finite-network artefact, but persists in the macroscopic continuum description considered here.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that higher-order (triplet) interactions emerging from adaptive network dynamics are not finite-size artifacts—they persist in the continuum limit, and the order of reduction vs. continuum limit does not matter up to controlled error.
AI.motivation: To determine whether effective higher-order phase coupling terms, arising from a slow-manifold reduction in adaptive oscillator networks, survive the dense-graph continuum limit, and whether the order of operations (reduce then take continuum limit, or vice versa) affects the result.
AI.method: The authors prove well-posedness and discrete-to-continuum convergence for unreduced and first-order reduced models; construct the continuum slow manifold in a Banach space; compare the two routes (reduce-then-limit vs. limit-then-reduce) along admissible equal-cell step approximations; and apply a mixed-derivative criterion to characterize the resulting triplet operator.
AI.result: Both routes yield the same first-order continuum vector field, including the same pairwise correction and triplet operator, up to controlled O(ε²) remainders. The triplet operator is genuinely nonpairwise in the smooth bounded-kernel class for suitable coupling functions.
AI.conclusion: Higher-order interactions are not finite-network artifacts but persist in the macroscopic continuum description, confirming their fundamental role in adaptive network dynamics.

title: Hierarchical Framework of Runaway Electrons using Deep Learning
summary: We present an adjoint deep learning framework describing the evolution of fluid moments and the energy distribution of the runaway electron (RE) population. We demonstrate that a careful formulation of the adjoint problem allows for the temporal evolution of these quantities for arbitrary initial electron distributions, and in combination with a physics-informed neural network (PINN), we show that the resulting surrogates can resolve a broad range of plasma parameters. This combination of the adjoint formulation and rapid inference of neural networks enables orders of magnitude faster predictions of RE kinetics than traditional methods. Here, we detail the mathematical formulation and the design of three PINNs which recover the temporal evolution of the RE current, average energy and energy distribution. Predictions are validated against a traditional RE solver, with good agreement across a broad range of scenarios.

AI.tldr: An adjoint deep learning framework combining physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) with adjoint formulations to rapidly predict runaway electron kinetics, achieving orders-of-magnitude speedup over traditional solvers while maintaining accuracy across broad plasma parameters.
AI.motivation: To accelerate predictions of runaway electron (RE) kinetics (current, average energy, energy distribution) in plasma physics, as traditional solvers are computationally expensive and slow.
AI.method: Combines an adjoint formulation (for temporal evolution of fluid moments from arbitrary initial distributions) with physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) as surrogates. Three PINNs are designed to recover RE current, average energy, and energy distribution over time.
AI.result: Orders-of-magnitude faster predictions than traditional RE solvers, with good agreement validated across a broad range of plasma scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The adjoint-PINN framework is an effective and efficient surrogate for modeling RE kinetics, enabling rapid inference while preserving physical accuracy.

title: The three dimensional Neumann Green's function for general surfaces: singular asymptotics and boundary integral methods
summary: We present an asymptotic analysis and high-order boundary integral method for the three-dimensional Neumann Green's function in general geometries. The Neumann Green's function is a fundamental quantity which arises in numerous fields of science and engineering. In the application of singular perturbation methods to strongly localized reactions and diffusive transport, the Green's function plays the key role in mediating global dynamics. However, this essential quantity can only be determined in closed form for a limited set of geometries. The Green's function for the Laplacian is an elliptic problem with a Dirac forcing term. Accurate resolution of the solution requires a careful decomposition into a singular and a regular part. The bulk scenario is where the source is placed off surface and the singularity is given by the free-space function. In the surface case, where the source is placed at a curved point on the boundary, we use asymptotic analysis to determine a three-term singularity structure. With explicit knowledge of these singularities, we develop a high-order boundary integral method for the determination of the remaining regular part. To resolve the singular boundary data, our integral method uses a custom discretization with Duffy patches near the source. We validate our method using several test cases in which closed form solutions can be developed, including spheres, prolate spheroids and constructed domains. We demonstrate the applicability of our method to address some open problems in narrow capture theory.

AI.tldr: A high-order boundary integral method with asymptotic analysis is developed for computing the 3D Neumann Green's function in general geometries, handling both bulk and surface source singularities, validated on spheres, spheroids, and applied to narrow capture theory problems.
AI.motivation: The Neumann Green's function is crucial for modeling localized reactions and diffusive transport, but closed-form solutions exist only for limited geometries, motivating a numerical method for general geometries.
AI.method: Decomposes solution into singular (free-space or asymptotic three-term for surface sources) and regular parts; uses high-order boundary integral method with Duffy patches for resolving singular boundary data near the source.
AI.result: Validated on spheres, prolate spheroids, and constructed domains where closed-form solutions exist; demonstrated applicability to open problems in narrow capture theory.
AI.conclusion: The method enables accurate computation of Neumann Green's functions in arbitrary 3D geometries, extending capabilities for singular perturbation problems in diffusive transport.

title: REMAL: Residual Equilibrium Manifold Active Learning for Surrogate-Based Multidisciplinary Design Analysis
summary: Multidisciplinary design analysis of coupled engineering systems requires the computation of equilibrium states in which all disciplinary coupling variables are mutually consistent. Conventional fixed-point iteration resolves this consistency problem separately at each design point, which can become expensive when disciplinary evaluations are costly and many analyses are required in outer-loop tasks such as multidisciplinary design optimization, uncertainty quantification, or digital twin updating. This paper introduces REMAL, a residual manifold surrogate modeling framework for coupled systems. Instead of approximating each discipline independently or directly learning converged coupling variables, the proposed method learns a surrogate model of the joint residual manifold via multitask Gaussian process models. An entropy-based active learning strategy selects additional residual evaluations near uncertain zero-contour regions, and equilibrium states for new design inputs are recovered by solving a nonlinear least squares optimization problem using only the trained surrogate. The method is evaluated on four engineering coupled system benchmarks: a satellite model, an aerostructural model, a finite-element gas-turbine heat-transfer and economics model, and a modified turbine model with added feedback coupling. Across these cases, REMAL consistently demonstrates the cost effectiveness when repeated evaluations of the fixed point across the design space are necessary. Theoretically, we show that, under mild assumptions, REMAL's predictive fixed point error is bounded.

AI.tldr: REMAL is a surrogate modeling framework that learns a joint residual manifold via multitask Gaussian processes to efficiently find equilibrium states in coupled multidisciplinary systems, avoiding costly fixed-point iterations at every design point.
AI.motivation: Conventional fixed-point iteration for achieving coupling consistency in multidisciplinary systems is computationally expensive, especially when many analyses are required in outer-loop tasks like MDO, UQ, or digital twin updating.
AI.method: REMAL builds a multitask Gaussian process surrogate of the joint residual manifold, uses entropy-based active learning to select evaluations near uncertain zero-contour regions, and recovers equilibrium states via nonlinear least squares optimization on the surrogate.
AI.result: Evaluated on four benchmarks (satellite, aerostructural, turbine heat-transfer/economics, modified turbine with feedback), REMAL consistently shows cost-effectiveness for repeated fixed-point evaluations across the design space.
AI.conclusion: Under mild assumptions, REMAL's predictive fixed-point error is theoretically bounded, making it a reliable and efficient alternative for coupled system analysis in outer-loop tasks.

title: The formation of gradient-driven singular structures of codimension one and two in two-dimensions: The case study of ferronematics. Part~{II}: Refined structure of the energy-concentration set
comment: This paper is Part II of arXiv:2505.07506
summary: In this paper, we continue our study, started in~\cite{CDS1}, of a two-dimensional variational model for ferronematics -- composite materials formed by dispersing magnetic nanoparticles into a liquid crystal matrix. The model features two coupled order parameters: a Landau-de Gennes~$\Q$-tensor for the liquid crystal component and a magnetisation vector field~$\M$, both of them governed by a Ginzburg-Landau-type energy. The energy includes a singular coupling term favouring alignment between~$\Q$ and~$\M$. We analyse the asymptotic behaviour of (not necessarily minimizing) critical points as a small parameter~$\eps$ tends to zero. While in~\cite{CDS1} we showed that the (rescaled) energy density for the~$\Q$-component concentrates, to leading order, on a finite number of singular points, in this paper we prove the energy density for the~$\M$-component concentrates along a one-dimensional rectifiable set. Moreover, we prove that the curvature of the singular set for the $\M$-component (technically, the first variation of the associated varifold) is concentrated on a finite number of points, i.e.~the singular set for the~$\Q$-component. Crucial to our arguments will be the energy estimates and compactness results proved in~\cite{CDS1}.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes how critical points in a ferronematic model concentrate as a parameter ε→0: the Q-tensor (liquid crystal) energy concentrates at finite points, while the magnetization M energy concentrates along a 1D set, with curvature of M's singular set localized at the Q-tensor's singular points.
AI.motivation: To understand the asymptotic behavior of coupled order parameters in ferronematics—composites of magnetic nanoparticles in liquid crystals—specifically how singularities distribute between the liquid crystal (Q-tensor) and magnetic (M) components in the vanishing ε limit.
AI.method: Asymptotic analysis of (non-minimizing) critical points of a Ginzburg-Landau-type energy coupling a Landau-de Gennes Q-tensor with a magnetization vector field M, including a singular alignment term. Relies on energy estimates and compactness results from previous work (CDS1).
AI.result: (1) The rescaled energy density for the M-component concentrates along a one-dimensional rectifiable set. (2) The curvature (first variation of the varifold) of M's singular set is concentrated on a finite number of points—exactly the singular set of the Q-component.
AI.conclusion: The Q-tensor singularities (points) act as "anchors" for the M-component's line singularities, establishing a hierarchical structure: 0D defects in the liquid crystal govern the geometry of 1D defects in the magnetization.

title: The toroidal flux and separatrix effects in tokamaks
summary: The toroidal magnetic flux enclosed by magnetic surface has remarkably little prominence in the tokamak literature. Nevertheless, it is required to properly interpret Faraday's Law, which gives the slippage of the poloidal relative to the toroidal magnetic flux through the loop voltage. The toroidal flux also simplifies and makes more precise the equilibrium conditions when a tokamak plasma is bounded by a separatrix. This paper illustrates the effect of a separatrix on equilibria using an analytic model in which the $z$ Cartesian coordinate is assumed to define a periodicity direction. Using 95\% of the poloidal flux between the axis and the separatrix to define the edge rotational transform, $q_{95}$, gives the definition an unnecessary sensitivity to the current profile in the central part of the plasma. The required avoidance of disruptions places great importance on identifying parameters that can be both measured and controlled in tokamak power plants. This identification would be clarified by the use of the toroidal rather than the poloidal flux as the radial coordinate.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that toroidal magnetic flux is underutilized in tokamak research despite its importance for interpreting Faraday's Law, equilibrium conditions near a separatrix, and disruption avoidance. It proposes using toroidal flux as a radial coordinate and critiques the common use of 95% poloidal flux for defining edge rotational transform.
AI.motivation: The toroidal magnetic flux has been largely overlooked in tokamak literature, yet it is essential for properly applying Faraday's Law and improving equilibrium descriptions near a separatrix.
AI.method: An analytic model is used where the z Cartesian coordinate defines a periodicity direction to illustrate the effect of a separatrix on equilibria.
AI.result: Using 95% of poloidal flux to define q95 introduces unnecessary sensitivity to the central current profile. Toroidal flux is better suited as a radial coordinate for clarifying measurable and controllable parameters in tokamak power plants.
AI.conclusion: Adopting toroidal flux as the radial coordinate would improve the identification of key parameters for disruption avoidance in tokamak power plants.

title: A variable time-step, second-order, and MBP-preserving linear stabilized scheme for the time-fractional Allen-Cahn equation
summary: In this paper, we present a second-order linear scheme based on the variable-step Alikhanov formula and central difference discretization for the time-fractional Allen-Cahn equation. The nonlinear potential is treated explicitly via a second-order extrapolation with preprocessing, which enables the discrete maximum-bound principle (MBP) to be preserved through an appropriate stabilization technique. Moreover, by developing a discrete fractional Grönwall inequality together with the uniform boundedness of numerical solutions guaranteed by the MBP, we establish an $α$-robust and optimal second-order maximum-norm error estimate under initial weak singularity assumption. In addition, energy stability is proved in the sense that the discrete original energy is uniformly bounded by the initial energy plus a high-order spatiotemporal correction term. Finally, extensive numerical experiments are presented to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed scheme.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a second-order linear numerical scheme for the time-fractional Allen-Cahn equation that preserves the discrete maximum-bound principle, achieves α-robust optimal error estimates, and maintains energy stability.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient and stable numerical method for the time-fractional Allen-Cahn equation that preserves key physical properties (maximum-bound principle, energy stability) while achieving second-order accuracy under initial weak singularities.
AI.method: A variable-step Alikhanov formula combined with central difference discretization, explicit second-order extrapolation for nonlinear potential, stabilization technique for MBP preservation, and a discrete fractional Grönwall inequality for error analysis.
AI.result: The scheme preserves the discrete maximum-bound principle, yields α-robust and optimal second-order maximum-norm error estimates, and proves energy stability with uniform boundedness of numerical solutions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed scheme is effective and theoretically sound for solving the time-fractional Allen-Cahn equation, as demonstrated by extensive numerical experiments.

title: Multiplicity Results for the Nonlinear Weighted Laplacian Equation on the Sierpiński Gasket
summary: In this paper we consider the Dirichlet boundary value problem driven by the weighted Laplacian equation and considered on the Sierpiński gasket. Under suitable growth conditions imposed on the nonlinear term, we provide existence and multiplicity results employing the direct method, mountain pass theorem and some related multiplicity result.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the Dirichlet boundary value problem for weighted Laplacian equations on the Sierpiński gasket, proving existence and multiplicity of solutions using variational methods.
AI.motivation: To extend the analysis of weighted Laplacian equations to fractal domains (specifically the Sierpiński gasket), where classical PDE theory may not directly apply.
AI.method: The paper employs the direct method of calculus of variations, the mountain pass theorem, and a related multiplicity result to prove existence and multiplicity of solutions under suitable growth conditions on the nonlinear term.
AI.result: Existence and multiplicity results for solutions to the Dirichlet boundary value problem for weighted Laplacian equations on the Sierpiński gasket.
AI.conclusion: Under appropriate growth conditions, multiple solutions exist for this boundary value problem on fractal domains, validated by variational techniques.

title: Enhanced Target Interaction Area of Helical Plasma Plumes in a Pulsed RF Atmospheric Plasma Jet
summary: Helical plasma plumes generated in pulsed radiofrequency atmospheric pressure plasma jets exhibit unique flow dynamics arising from the coupling between Kelvin-Helmholtz instabilities and baroclinic torque. While the fundamental mechanism responsible for helical plume formation has been established, the implications of this morphology for plasma-target interaction remain largely unexplored. In this study, we experimentally investigate the interaction area of helical plasma plumes with different target configurations and compare it with the conventional conical plume produced under continuous RF excitation. High-speed imaging reveals that the helical plume significantly enlarges the effective plasma-target interaction region due to its rotating trajectory and enhanced air entrainment. The effect of different target materials and boundary conditions, including dielectric surfaces and metal-backed substrates, is systematically examined. The results demonstrate that helical plumes provide superior surface coverage, improved spatial distribution of reactive species, and enhanced plasma-surface coupling. These findings highlight the advantages of helical plasma jets for applications requiring large-area plasma treatment, including plasma medicine, surface modification, and plasma-liquid interactions.

AI.tldr: Helical plasma plumes generated by pulsed RF atmospheric pressure plasma jets create larger interaction areas and better surface coverage than conventional conical plumes due to their rotating trajectory and enhanced air entrainment, offering advantages for large-area plasma treatment applications.
AI.motivation: While the formation mechanism of helical plumes via Kelvin-Helmholtz instabilities and baroclinic torque is known, their implications for plasma-target interaction have not been explored.
AI.method: Experimental investigation using high-speed imaging to compare helical vs. conical plumes on various target materials (dielectric surfaces, metal-backed substrates), analyzing interaction area and spatial distribution.
AI.result: Helical plumes significantly enlarge effective plasma-target interaction region, provide superior surface coverage, improved spatial distribution of reactive species, and enhanced plasma-surface coupling compared to conventional conical plumes.
AI.conclusion: Helical plasma jets offer advantages for applications requiring large-area treatment, such as plasma medicine, surface modification, and plasma-liquid interactions.

title: Massively parallel flow routing and drainage area determination
summary: Digital elevation models (DEMs) have reached resolutions and sizes that only parallel computaters can efficiently process. One important application of DEMs is predicting how much water flows where, the so-called ``flow routing problem'' (a variation of which is the problem of determining the drainage area upstream of a point in a DEM). The traditional algorithm for flow routing is sequential, and attempts to parallelize this method have so far only been moderately successful. Herein, we build on earlier work in Richardson et al. (2014) and propose an algorithm and several variations that can efficiently solve the flow routing problem on very large models with very large numbers of parallel processes. For the largest model we use, with 1.88 billion points, the best algorithm herein can route water in 4.0 seconds on 12,288 processes of a computer cluster.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a parallel algorithm for flow routing on very large digital elevation models, achieving 4.0 seconds on 12,288 processes for 1.88 billion points.
AI.motivation: DEMs have grown too large for sequential processing of flow routing, and existing parallelization attempts have been only moderately successful.
AI.method: The authors build on prior work (Richardson et al., 2014) and propose an algorithm with several variations designed for efficient parallel computation on very large DEMs using many parallel processes.
AI.result: The best algorithm variant processes a 1.88 billion-point DEM in 4.0 seconds using 12,288 processes on a computer cluster.
AI.conclusion: The proposed algorithm efficiently solves the flow routing problem at scale, demonstrating strong parallel performance on massive DEM datasets.

title: Molecular reference corrections for quantum Monte Carlo adsorption energies
comment: 34 pages, 2 figures, 3 main tables; Supplemental Material included
summary: Accurate surface thermochemistry requires balanced error cancellation between extended slabs and molecular reference states. This balance can fail whenever the electronic-structure error is not transferable across the chemically distinct species entering a thermodynamic cycle. Here we examine this problem in single-determinant fixed-node diffusion Monte Carlo (SD-FNDMC) for oxygenated ORR intermediates on Pt(111). Gas-phase thermochemistry is used to diagnose the reference-state imbalance, and a hybrid cycle is introduced to separate slab-adsorbate binding from molecular formation. The hybrid cycle keeps the surface binding term at the SD-FNDMC level, where cancellation is expected to be most favorable, and replaces the molecular formation contribution with a benchmark coupled cluster reference. For Pt(111), the resulting correction is small for O and OH but larger for OOH, while the geometry-matched refinement gives only a secondary correction. Applying the same cycle to HCO and COH on Cu(111) gives corrections of opposite sign, showing that the bias is controlled primarily by the electronic structure of the molecular reference rather than by adsorbate geometry alone. This decomposition identifies molecular reference imbalance as a separable source of error in SD-FNDMC surface thermochemistry and reduces the corresponding bias without modifying the SD-FNDMC slab-binding contribution.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that inaccuracies in surface thermochemistry from SD-FNDMC calculations arise from imbalanced error cancellation between molecular reference states and slab calculations. A hybrid cycle replacing the molecular formation term with a coupled-cluster benchmark corrects this bias, with the correction magnitude depending on the electronic structure of the molecular species rather than adsorbate geometry.
AI.motivation: Accurate surface thermochemistry requires balanced error cancellation between extended slabs and molecular reference states, but this balance can fail when electronic-structure error is not transferable across chemically distinct species. The authors aim to diagnose and correct this reference-state imbalance in SD-FNDMC for oxygenated ORR intermediates on Pt(111).
AI.method: The authors use gas-phase thermochemistry to diagnose reference-state imbalance and introduce a hybrid cycle that separates slab-adsorbate binding (kept at SD-FNDMC level) from molecular formation (replaced with benchmark coupled cluster reference). They test this on Pt(111) for O, OH, OOH and on Cu(111) for HCO and COH.
AI.result: On Pt(111), the correction is small for O and OH but larger for OOH. The geometry-matched refinement gives only secondary correction. On Cu(111), corrections for HCO and COH have opposite signs, showing bias is controlled primarily by the molecular reference electronic structure rather than adsorbate geometry.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid cycle identifies molecular reference imbalance as a separable source of error in SD-FNDMC surface thermochemistry and reduces the corresponding bias without modifying the SD-FNDMC slab-binding contribution.

title: Invisible Defect Cascades for Navier-Stokes Regularity
summary: We formulate a conditional scale-critical defect-cascade reduction for the local regularity problem of the three-dimensional incompressible Navier--Stokes equations. The theorem concerns a potential singular point for which no sufficiently small dyadic scale enters the Caffarelli--Kohn--Nirenberg smallness regime. Under the structural hypotheses of the framework, such a point cannot be explained by an undifferentiated concentration of energy or pressure. It must lead either to non-effective moving-window observability or to an NS-realizable, cleaned, scale-critical defect cascade invisible to the combined active-pressure, flux, energy, and adjoint-trace tests. The reduction is built from dyadic rescaling, coarse graining, active/harmonic pressure splitting, Reynolds covariance positivity, pressure compatibility, and local energy-flux identities. Finite-window observability reduces possible invisible directions to explicitly defined residual kernels, while budget compatibility and sign coherence convert visible pressure--flux activity into depletion. Consequently, within a controlled window class where dyadic defect extraction, observable depletion, and moving-window growth control hold, effective observability together with exclusion of NS-realizable combined-invisible cascades yields a CKN scale and hence local regularity. The final component interprets the remaining obstruction as a critical recurrence problem and proposes diagnostics for vortex stretching, active pressure work, and interscale flux. Spatially harmonic pressure is retained as a physical local pressure component; only purely time-dependent pressure functions are treated as gauge.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a rigorous mathematical framework using conditional scale-critical defect-cascade reduction to analyze potential singular points in the 3D Navier-Stokes equations, showing that under specific structural hypotheses, either moving-window observability fails or an invisible defect cascade occurs, and establishes conditions under which local regularity is guaranteed via CKN scale theory.
AI.motivation: To address the local regularity problem of the 3D incompressible Navier-Stokes equations by identifying and characterizing the precise obstructions to regularity at potential singular points that evade the Caffarelli-Kohn-Nirenberg smallness criterion.
AI.method: The method employs dyadic rescaling, coarse graining, active/harmonic pressure splitting, Reynolds covariance positivity, pressure compatibility, and local energy-flux identities to build a scale-critical defect-cascade reduction framework.
AI.result: Within a controlled window class where dyadic defect extraction, observable depletion, and moving-window growth control hold, effective observability together with exclusion of NS-realizable combined-invisible cascades yields a CKN scale and hence local regularity.
AI.conclusion: The remaining obstruction is interpreted as a critical recurrence problem, with proposed diagnostics for vortex stretching, active pressure work, and interscale flux; spatially harmonic pressure is retained as physical while only purely time-dependent pressure functions are treated as gauge.

title: Ultrashort Pulse Train Generation on a 100TW Laser Beamline Using a Delay Mask After the Final Focusing Optics
summary: Experimental results aimed at demonstrating the feasibility of a two-section delay mask for the generation of ultrashort pulse trains are reported. Based on the initial simulation results, a 500 um thick circular fused silica plate featuring a central aperture was designed to enable two distinct transverse portions of the incident laser pulse to be focused, ideally, with equal intensity. This fulfills one of the requirements of the resonant multipulse ionization injection (ReMPI) scheme for laser wakefield acceleration. The experiment was carried out at the CNR-INO Intense Laser Irradiation Laboratory using a 240 TW laser system operated at 120 TW, as part of the ongoing preparation for the first experimental demonstration of ReMPI.

AI.tldr: This paper experimentally demonstrates a two-section delay mask using a fused silica plate with a central aperture to generate ultrashort pulse trains for the ReMPI scheme in laser wakefield acceleration, tested with a 120 TW laser system.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate the feasibility of a two-section delay mask for generating ultrashort pulse trains needed for the resonant multipulse ionization injection (ReMPI) scheme in laser wakefield acceleration.
AI.method: A 500 μm thick circular fused silica plate with a central aperture was designed based on simulations to split the incident laser pulse into two transverse portions with equal intensity, and tested at 120 TW power at the CNR-INO laser facility.
AI.result: Experimental results confirmed the feasibility of the two-section delay mask design, achieving focused laser pulses with equal intensity in two distinct transverse portions.
AI.conclusion: The two-section delay mask approach is viable for generating ultrashort pulse trains, supporting further experimental demonstration of the ReMPI scheme.

title: Local Consistency and Higher-Order Structure of Spherical Interpolation
summary: Spherical Interpolation of orDER $n$ (SIDER-$n$) is a recursive high-order interpolation construction for data on the unit sphere $\mathbb{S}^2$, built from repeated spherical linear interpolation (SLERP). This paper gives a local consistency analysis of SIDER for smooth spherical curves sampled at equally spaced parameter values. The analysis is carried out in geodesic normal coordinates, which allows the SIDER recursion to be compared with classical Neville interpolation while retaining the curvature-dependent corrections introduced by SLERP. We first derive local expansions of SLERP and show that SIDER2 has third-order accuracy; its leading error has the same shifted nodal structure as Euclidean quadratic interpolation. We then prove that the adjacent SIDER2 errors entering SIDER3 have a common leading coefficient, so that the SIDER3 recurrence cancels the cubic term and yields fourth-order accuracy. Carrying the expansion one order further gives the corresponding coefficient compatibility for SIDER3 and proves fifth-order accuracy of SIDER4. Finally, we introduce a degree-filtered formal expansion framework for the general SIDER recursion. This framework proves that, for each fixed $n$, SIDER-$n$ preserves the required polynomial degree structure in the normalized stencil variable. Together with the interpolation conditions at the $n+1$ nodes, this yields the local consistency estimate $d_{\mathbb{S}^2}\bigl(γ(θh),P_i^{[n]}(θ;h)\bigr)=O(h^{n+1})$ under the stated smoothness and small-stencil assumptions.

AI.tldr: SIDER-n is a recursive spherical interpolation method. This paper proves its local accuracy: SIDER2 is O(h³), SIDER3 is O(h⁴), SIDER4 is O(h⁵), and in general SIDER-n achieves O(h^{n+1}) consistency on the sphere.
AI.motivation: To provide a rigorous local consistency analysis of SIDER-n for smooth spherical curves sampled at equally spaced parameter values, establishing its order of accuracy.
AI.method: The analysis uses geodesic normal coordinates to compare SIDER recursion with classical Neville interpolation while incorporating curvature corrections from SLERP. Local expansions of SLERP are derived, and a degree-filtered formal expansion framework is introduced for the general recursion.
AI.result: SIDER2 achieves third-order accuracy, SIDER3 achieves fourth-order accuracy, SIDER4 achieves fifth-order accuracy, and general SIDER-n achieves O(h^{n+1}) local consistency.
AI.conclusion: Under smoothness and small-stencil assumptions, SIDER-n preserves the required polynomial degree structure in the normalized stencil variable, yielding the stated local consistency estimate.

title: Continuum Neural Momentum Eigenstate for Variationally Solving Quasiparticles
summary: We design the first neural quantum state for continuum particles that, for any chosen allowed momentum $\mathbf{k}$, is by construction an exact eigenstate of total momentum with eigenvalue $\mathbf{k}$. Our architecture, EVE, enables off-the-shelf VMC to solve for momentum-sector ground states. We test EVE on 2D bosons with mutual $1/r$ interactions, finding that a single unified ansatz is capable of describing four qualitatively different states: superfluid, roton, crystal, and phonon. At different densities, we extract the underlying phase of matter from the dispersion's shape. At $r_s = 20.0$, we see the roton minimum at finite $k$ expected of a superfluid. At $r_s = 100.0$, we see striking zone folding indicative of crystalline order, with periodically spaced minima representing floating crystals connected by phonon arcs in between. Using density-density correlation functions, we confirm the phase diagnoses and probe the excitations' correlation structures. Finally, we analyze the roton's phase texture and find unexpected multi-particle phase strings, formed when several vortex dipoles merge, leaving two vortices connected by a phase slip.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EVE, the first neural quantum state architecture for continuum particles that guarantees exact total momentum eigenstates, enabling Variational Monte Carlo to solve for momentum-sector ground states. Applied to 2D bosons with 1/r interactions, it captures four distinct phases (superfluid, roton, crystal, phonon) and reveals novel multi-particle phase strings from merging vortex dipoles.
AI.motivation: To develop a neural quantum state ansatz for continuum particles that can target specific momentum sectors by construction, enabling the study of excited states and dispersion relations without symmetry-breaking or post-processing.
AI.method: The EVE architecture is designed so that any allowed momentum k yields an exact eigenstate of total momentum. It is trained via off-the-shelf VMC to find momentum-sector ground states. The system studied is 2D bosons with mutual 1/r interactions.
AI.result: A single unified ansatz describes four qualitatively different states: superfluid, roton, crystal, and phonon. At r_s=20.0, a roton minimum is observed. At r_s=100.0, zone folding indicates crystalline order with phonon arcs. Density-density correlations confirm phase diagnoses. Unexpected multi-particle phase strings are found in the roton's phase texture, formed by merging vortex dipoles.
AI.conclusion: EVE successfully enables momentum-resolved neural quantum state calculations for continuum systems, revealing rich phase behavior and novel excitation structures (phase strings) in interacting boson systems.

title: On the weak formulation of Prandtl's minimum drag problem
summary:
We study Prandtl's classical problem on minimising the induced drag for a finite wing with fixed span. The induced drag is given by a singular quadratic functional of the circulation, with admissible functions satisfying the prescribed lift and second-moment conditions. We formulate the problem in the fractional Sobolev space \(H^{1/2}\), which is the natural energy space for the functional, prove existence and uniqueness of minimisers by variational methods, and derive the corresponding Euler--Lagrange equation. %
Passing to a periodic formulation on the one-dimensional torus, we identify the drag functional with the quadratic form of the half-Laplacian and solve the resulting singular integral equation explicitly and recover Prandtl's bell-shaped circulation profile.

AI.tldr: This paper revisits Prandtl's induced drag minimization problem using fractional Sobolev space H^{1/2}, proves existence and uniqueness of minimizers via variational methods, derives Euler-Lagrange equations, and recovers Prandtl's bell-shaped circulation profile through a periodic formulation on the torus.
AI.motivation: To rigorously formulate and solve Prandtl's classical induced drag minimization problem within the modern framework of fractional Sobolev spaces, establishing a mathematically sound foundation for the bell-shaped circulation profile.
AI.method: Formulates the problem in H^{1/2} (the natural energy space), uses variational methods to prove existence/uniqueness, derives the Euler-Lagrange equation, and solves the singular integral equation via a periodic formulation on the one-dimensional torus using the half-Laplacian.
AI.result: Successfully proves existence and uniqueness of minimizers and explicitly recovers Prandtl's bell-shaped circulation profile.
AI.conclusion: The H^{1/2} framework provides a rigorous and natural setting for Prandtl's induced drag minimization, bridging classical aerodynamics with modern functional analysis.

title: Archê, an orbital-free molecular dynamics code for fast production of equations of state
summary: We present Archê, an orbital-free molecular dynamics (OFMD) code designed to produce equations of state (EOS) in the plasma state. Unlike in other OFMD codes, Archê uses a self-consistent field (SCF) approach to compute the electronic density. This allows us to implement two algorithms that accelerate SCF convergence by a factor of up to six. First, the density is initialized using the results from the previous MD timestep to define a one-center profile, which is then applied to the new nuclei positions. Second, the initial and final densities are mixed at each SCF iteration in a proportion that minimizes an approximate free energy. We validate the code by comparing the calculated aluminum EOS to results obtained with the Kohn-Sham density functional theory software Abinit. Achieving agreement in the internal energy requires adding a correction related to the norm-conserving pseudopotential derived from an average-atom model. Performance is compared across CPU and GPU architectures, demonstrating an order-of-magnitude speedup for a single GPU compared to 256 CPUs. Archê exhibits an overall linear computational complexity with respect to the number of atoms, as well as the number of real and reciprocal grid points. Execution time is weakly dependent on density; however, interestingly, it decreases as temperature increases -- in contrast to simulations based on Kohn-Sham orbitals.

AI.tldr: Archê is a new orbital-free molecular dynamics code that accelerates self-consistent field convergence up to 6x via two algorithms, achieves linear complexity, runs faster on GPUs than 256 CPUs by an order of magnitude, and shows decreasing execution time with increasing temperature — validated against aluminum EOS from Kohn-Sham DFT.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient orbital-free molecular dynamics code capable of producing accurate equations of state in the plasma state, overcoming convergence and performance bottlenecks of existing OFMD and Kohn-Sham DFT approaches.
AI.method: Implements SCF-based electronic density computation with two acceleration algorithms: (1) one-center profile density initialization from previous MD timestep, (2) mixing initial and final densities at each SCF iteration to minimize approximate free energy. Uses norm-conserving pseudopotential with average-atom correction.
AI.result: SCF convergence accelerated by up to 6x; aluminum EOS matches Abinit Kohn-Sham DFT results with pseudopotential correction; single GPU achieves order-of-magnitude speedup over 256 CPUs; overall O(N) complexity; execution time decreases with increasing temperature.
AI.conclusion: Archê provides a highly efficient, scalable OFMD solution for plasma EOS calculations, with unique advantages in temperature scaling and computational cost compared to orbital-based methods.

title: A Quaternion--BCH Framework for the Local Accuracy of SIDER Interpolation
summary: Spherical Interpolation of orDER $n$ (SIDER-$n$) is a recursive high-order interpolation method for data on the unit sphere $\mathbb{S}^2$, built from repeated spherical linear interpolation (SLERP). This paper develops a quaternion--Lie algebra framework for proving the local consistency of SIDER for smooth spherical curves sampled at equally spaced parameter values. Points on $\mathbb{S}$ are represented as pure unit quaternions, and interpolation errors are measured in fixed-base quaternion logarithmic coordinates. In this setting, each SLERP operation admits an exact Baker--Campbell--Hausdorff (BCH) representation, which converts the geometric interpolation problem into an algebraic problem involving filtered Lie-polynomial expansions. The BCH expansion shows that SLERP is affine to leading order, has no quadratic correction, and has a first nonlinear correction that is cubic and commutator-valued. Using this structure, we prove that SIDER2 has a third-order divided-error form with the same leading nodal factor as ordinary quadratic interpolation. We then show that the recursive SIDER step raises the order by one: the affine part gives the Neville-type finite-difference cancellation, while the nonlinear BCH remainder preserves the sharp filtered degree structure after the nodal factor is removed. Consequently, for every fixed $n\geq2$, $d_{\mathbb{S}^2}\bigl(γ(θh),P_i^{[n]}(θ;h)\bigr) = O(h^{n+1}) $under the stated smoothness and small-stencil assumptions. The proof also identifies the shift-invariance of the leading divided-error coefficient as the algebraic compatibility condition underlying the SIDER recurrence.

AI.tldr: SIDER-n is a recursive high-order spherical interpolation method. Using a quaternion–Lie algebra framework with BCH expansions, the paper proves that SIDER-n achieves O(h^{n+1}) local consistency for smooth curves sampled on the unit sphere.
AI.motivation: To establish rigorous local consistency guarantees for SIDER, a recursive high-order interpolation method on the unit sphere, by developing a quaternion–Lie algebra framework.
AI.method: Represent points as pure unit quaternions, measure errors in fixed-base logarithmic coordinates, apply exact BCH expansions to SLERP, and use filtered Lie-polynomial expansions to analyze error propagation in the recursive SIDER scheme.
AI.result: SIDER2 has third-order divided-error form; the recursive step raises order by one; overall error is O(h^{n+1}) for smooth, equally spaced data under small-stencil assumptions.
AI.conclusion: The quaternion–Lie algebra framework proves high-order local consistency of SIDER, with shift-invariance of the leading divided-error coefficient as the algebraic compatibility condition underlying the recurrence.

title: Self-similar imploding solutions of the 1D compressible Euler equations with a far field cutoff
summary: Imploding solutions to the radially symmetric, isentropic, compressible Euler equations have been well-studied, inspired by the work of Guderley. However, these smooth imploding solutions are shown to be numerically unstable and difficult to compute in practice. On the other hand, the imploding solution of Kidder has a closed form solution and is numerically computable. But, it is unbounded in the far field. We consider Kidder's formulation in one dimension in which the unbounded far field condition is replaced with a constant density cutoff of the initial data. Strikingly, a non-centered rarefaction emerges from the cutoff and suppresses the implosion. We present an exact analytic solution to the problem with the cutoff and support our theoretical predictions with numerical simulations.

AI.tldr: This paper studies Kidder's imploding solution for compressible Euler equations, replacing the unbounded far-field condition with a constant density cutoff. The authors find that a non-centered rarefaction emerges from the cutoff, suppressing the implosion. They present an exact analytic solution and validate it with numerical simulations.
AI.motivation: Smooth imploding solutions to the compressible Euler equations (Guderley-type) are numerically unstable and hard to compute. Kidder's closed-form solution is computable but unbounded in the far field. This work aims to address this far-field issue while maintaining solvability.
AI.method: The authors modify Kidder's 1D formulation by replacing the unbounded far-field condition with a constant density cutoff in the initial data. They then derive an exact analytic solution for this modified problem.
AI.result: A non-centered rarefaction wave emerges from the density cutoff, which suppresses the implosion. The authors provide an exact analytic solution and confirm their theoretical findings with numerical simulations.
AI.conclusion: Introducing a constant density cutoff in Kidder's formulation leads to a surprising physical phenomenon (rarefaction-suppressed implosion), which can be solved exactly analytically and validated numerically, offering a more practical and computable approach.

title: Feasibility of a Flexible, Hybrid Tokamak-Stellarator Experiment using an Axisymmetric Dipole Coil Array
summary: We demonstrate the design of a flexible, university-scale hybrid tokamak-stellarator experiment based on an axisymmetric array of planar HTS dipole coils. Because the coil array has few geometric degrees of freedom, we use single-stage optimization of the coil currents initialized from two-stage solutions to obtain mutually consistent equilibria and coil sets within realistic engineering limits. We find that the field error and coil current thresholds set minimum and maximum coil-plasma distances that confine the boundary to a roughly fixed axisymmetric envelope, within which rotational transform, volume, coil current, and quasi-symmetry (QS) error trade off against one another. Tighter current limits delocalize the non-axisymmetric shaping and raise QS error at fixed transform. From this single coil array we obtain a broad range of equilibria-quasi-axisymmetric vacuum stellarators with $ι$ up to 0.2, finite-$β$ hybrids with realistic profiles reaching on-axis $ι$ $\approx$ 1 and vacuum transform relevant for MHD stabilization, and strongly shaped tokamaks with elongation $κ$ = 1.7 and triangularity $δ$ = $\pm$0.6, all at peak pointwise coil forces well below the HTS tolerance. We show the same array can additionally correct toroidal field (TF) coil ripple, reducing the number of TF coils required compared to the equivalent tokamak. These results establish the design as a promising platform for hybrid tokamak-stellarator research.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a flexible, university-scale hybrid tokamak-stellarator experiment using a single axisymmetric array of planar HTS dipole coils, demonstrating a wide range of equilibria (quasi-axisymmetric stellarators, finite-β hybrids, shaped tokamaks) with realistic engineering constraints and low coil forces.
AI.motivation: To design a flexible, university-scale experimental platform that can explore both tokamak and stellarator physics regimes using a single coil configuration, enabling hybrid tokamak-stellarator research.
AI.method: Single-stage optimization of coil currents initialized from two-stage solutions; axisymmetric array of planar HTS dipole coils with few geometric degrees of freedom.
AI.result: Achieved quasi-axisymmetric vacuum stellarators (ι ≤ 0.2), finite-β hybrids (on-axis ι ≈ 1), and strongly shaped tokamaks (κ=1.7, δ=±0.6) with coil forces below HTS tolerance; demonstrated TF coil ripple correction.
AI.conclusion: The coil array design is a promising platform for hybrid tokamak-stellarator research, offering broad operational flexibility within realistic engineering limits.

title: A Robust Helmholtz-Decomposition-Based Real Compressed Layer Method for Time-Harmonic Elastic Wave Scattering
summary: Time-harmonic elastic wave scattering involves both compressional (P-) and shear (S-) waves, which propagate with different wavenumbers and polarization characteristics. The naive construction of perfectly matched layer (PML)-type methods based on complex coordinate stretching may lack robustness, or even fail, particularly when the wavenumbers are highly contrasted. The recently developed real compressed layer (RCL) technique build upon real compression transformations and explicit extraction of resulting oscillatory patterns for time-harmonic Helmholtz problems may not work, since the oscillations cannot be explicitly extracted by a single change of variables. This paper intends to bridge this gap by developing a robust RCL method for two-dimensional time-harmonic elastic wave scattering in unbounded domains with compactly supported inhomogeneities. A key observation is that, through the Helmholtz decomposition, the displacement field in the exterior homogeneous region decoupled into P-wave and S-wave and each has a distinctive separation of its oscillatory pattern and decaying behaviours in polar coordinates. We then apply the real compression coordinate transformation in the radial direction to each component. We further propose a coupled displacement-potential RCL formulation that seamlessly integrates the Helmholtz-decomposed wave components with the interior displacement field. We show that, under this framework, the essential oscillations in the layer can be effectively removed. We prove the well-posedness of the resulting coupled problem and establish the exponential convergence of the RCL solution to the original scattering solution in the truncated domain of interest. We discretize the RCL-system using high-order spectral element method and demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed method through ample numerical results.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a robust Real Compressed Layer (RCL) method for 2D time-harmonic elastic wave scattering, overcoming the limitations of PML methods when P- and S-waves have highly contrasted wavenumbers. By leveraging Helmholtz decomposition to decouple waves in polar coordinates and applying real compression transformations separately, the method effectively removes oscillations in the layer, with proven well-posedness and exponential convergence.
AI.motivation: Standard PML methods for elastic wave scattering can lack robustness or fail when P- and S-wavenumbers are highly contrasted. Existing RCL techniques for Helmholtz problems cannot be directly applied because the oscillations cannot be extracted by a single change of variables. There is a need for a robust method that handles the coupled nature of compressional and shear waves.
AI.method: The method uses Helmholtz decomposition to separate the displacement field in the exterior region into decoupled P-wave and S-wave components in polar coordinates. Real compression coordinate transformation is applied radially to each component. A coupled displacement-potential RCL formulation is proposed to integrate the decomposed wave components with the interior displacement field, effectively removing essential oscillations in the layer.
AI.result: The paper proves the well-posedness of the coupled problem and establishes exponential convergence of the RCL solution to the original scattering solution. Numerical results using high-order spectral element method demonstrate effectiveness and robustness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed RCL method robustly handles elastic wave scattering with highly contrasted wavenumbers, bridging a critical gap left by PML methods and prior RCL techniques. It offers exponential convergence and is validated through ample numerical experiments.

title: A beam--membrane biomechanical vocal fold model incorporating posturing and glottal conformation
summary: The posture of the vocal folds produced by laryngeal muscle activation plays a central role in determining the dynamics of voice production. Abnormal vocal fold configurations are frequently associated with inefficient phonation and a variety of voice disorders. Although diverse glottal closure patterns have been observed clinically, the biomechanical mechanisms governing their dynamic behavior and resulting phonatory characteristics remain incompletely understood. Moreover, existing numerical models that incorporate the effects of the intrinsic musculature on posturing and glottal conformation are computationally expensive, which limits their suitability for large-scale parametric investigations. In this work, we introduce a computationally inexpensive vocal fold (VF) model wherein the body and cover VF layers are treated as a composite beam and a coupled membrane, respectively. Intrinsic laryngeal muscle activation, in addition to positioning the arytenoid cartilages and cricothyroid joint, introduces moments at the boundaries of the structure that influence glottal conformation. The model produces phonatory characteristics that are qualitatively consistent with those reported in high-fidelity finite-element models and clinical studies, thereby supporting its predictive capability while offering substantial computational advantage. The proposed framework provides biomechanical insights into the influence of incomplete glottal closure on phonation dynamics and may serve as a computationally tractable tool for investigating mechanisms underlying certain voice disorders.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a computationally efficient vocal fold model that treats the body and cover layers as a composite beam and coupled membrane, incorporating laryngeal muscle activation to study phonatory dynamics, achieving results consistent with high-fidelity models while offering significant computational savings.
AI.motivation: Abnormal vocal fold configurations are linked to voice disorders, but existing high-fidelity numerical models are computationally too expensive for large-scale parametric studies, motivating the need for a faster yet accurate model.
AI.method: A composite beam (body) and coupled membrane (cover) representation of vocal folds, with intrinsic laryngeal muscle activation modeled as boundary moments affecting glottal conformation.
AI.result: The model produces phonatory characteristics qualitatively consistent with high-fidelity finite-element models and clinical observations, with substantial computational advantages.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers biomechanical insights into incomplete glottal closure effects on phonation and serves as a computationally tractable tool for studying voice disorder mechanisms.

title: Elliptic criticality versus Volterra memory in indirect chemotaxis cascades
summary: Indirect signal production is often treated as a higher-order variant of classical Keller-Segel chemotaxis, but its critical structure depends strongly on how the signal cascade is closed. This paper separates two asymptotic regimes of a two-stage signalling mechanism. In the parabolic--elliptic--elliptic limit, the chemoattractant is generated by the self-adjoint fourth-order operator $K_τ=(I-τΔ)^{-1}(I-Δ)^{-1}$. We prove its spectral positivity, entropy-dissipation structure, fourth-order principal scaling, and logarithmic kernel singularity in four dimensions. Consequently, the correct critical space is $L^{N/4}$, and $N=4$ is the mass-critical dimension. A concentration calculation identifies the natural threshold candidate $M_* = 64π^2τ/χ$, while the sharp threshold theorem is formulated as an Adams/logarithmic-HLS open problem. In contrast, the mixed elliptic--parabolic cascade cannot be reduced to a static fourth-order kernel. Its eliminated signal is a Volterra memory operator whose near-diagonal multiplier has the same order as the classical Keller-Segel drift. Thus its critical theory must be based on mixed space--time estimates, not static elliptic scaling. Numerical experiments support our operator-level distinction.

AI.tldr: This paper distinguishes two asymptotic regimes in two-stage chemotaxis signaling: one reduces to a fourth-order operator with L^{N/4} critical space and mass-critical dimension N=4, while the other requires mixed space-time estimates due to Volterra memory effects.
AI.motivation: To clarify how the closure of the signal cascade determines the critical structure of indirect signal production chemotaxis, which is often mistakenly treated as a simple variant of classical Keller-Segel.
AI.method: Mathematical analysis of two asymptotic regimes: parabolic-elliptic-elliptic limit (fourth-order operator K_τ) and mixed elliptic-parabolic cascade (Volterra memory operator). Spectral analysis, entropy-dissipation, scaling arguments, and numerical experiments.
AI.result: For the PEE limit: spectral positivity of K_τ, entropy-dissipation structure, fourth-order principal scaling, logarithmic kernel singularity in 4D, critical space L^{N/4}, N=4 is mass-critical, threshold M_* = 64π^2τ/χ. For the mixed cascade: Volterra memory operator with Keller-Segel order drift, requiring space-time estimates.
AI.conclusion: The two regimes are fundamentally different: the PEE limit is governed by static fourth-order elliptic theory, while the mixed cascade requires dynamic space-time analysis. Numerical experiments confirm this operator-level distinction.

title: Robust Control of ECH Deposition Profiles on DIII-D
summary: Electron Cyclotron Heating (ECH) is a key actuator in DIII-D and future tokamaks that provides auxiliary heating, localized current drive for scenario development and MHD stability, and even impurity pump-out. Due to its control flexibility and applications, a gyrotron optimization algorithm was developed to multitask and fine-tune the deposition location and heating power of each gyrotron while providing robustness to hardware failure. The ECH Optimization (ECHO) algorithm finds the optimal gyrotron mirror angle and power to achieve a target ECH radial deposition profile. This optimization is accomplished in real-time using a parallelized neural network surrogate of the TORBEAM code combined with a genetic optimizer. This has been deployed in a DIII-D experiment and has been validated with experimental ECE measurements and post-experiment offline ray tracing, showing the algorithm's reliability despite gyrotron failures and significant changes to plasma parameters.

AI.tldr: ECHO algorithm optimizes gyrotron mirror angles and power in real-time using neural network surrogates and genetic optimization to achieve target ECH deposition profiles, validated in DIII-D experiments with robustness to hardware failures.
AI.motivation: ECH is a critical actuator for heating, current drive, MHD stability, and impurity control in tokamaks, requiring an algorithm that can multitask, fine-tune deposition, and remain robust to hardware failures.
AI.method: A parallelized neural network surrogate of the TORBEAM code combined with a genetic optimizer to find optimal gyrotron mirror angles and power in real-time.
AI.result: Deployed and validated in DIII-D experiments using ECE measurements and post-experiment ray tracing; algorithm showed reliability despite gyrotron failures and plasma parameter changes.
AI.conclusion: ECHO provides a reliable, real-time optimization framework for ECH control with robustness to hardware failures and plasma variations.

title: Three-term Recurrence Relation with Arbitrary Degree Step for Orthogonal Polynomials
summary: An approach to generate three-term recurrence relations with arbitrary degree step is proposed. Specifically, given any class of orthogonal polynomials $\{Q_{p}(x)\}_{p=0}^{\infty}$ defined by Favard's theorem, we employ the adjacent members $Q_{p}(x)$ and $Q_{p-1}(x)$ to compute the one of high degree $Q_{p+s}(x)$ and that of low degree $Q_{p-t}(x)$, where $(s,t)\in \mathbb{N^{+}}$. Therefore, it is able to derive a three-term recurrence relation with respect to $Q_{p+s}(x)$, $Q_{p}(x)$ and $Q_{p-t}(x)$ by taking $Q_{p-1}(x)$ as the bridge. Moreover, as the extensions of standard recursive formula which is characterized with degree increase, the ones for degree decrease and end-to-middle directions are formulated as well. The explicit recurrence relations with two-degree step are offered for Hermite, Gegenbauer and Legendre polynomials. Precision comparison is also performed between the standard three-term recurrence relation and the proposed ones.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a method to generate three-term recurrence relations with arbitrary degree step for orthogonal polynomials, extending beyond the standard degree-increase formula to include degree-decrease and end-to-middle directions, with explicit examples for Hermite, Gegenbauer, and Legendre polynomials.
AI.motivation: To generalize the standard three-term recurrence relation for orthogonal polynomials beyond the conventional degree-increase step, enabling arbitrary step sizes in both forward and backward directions.
AI.method: Using Favard's theorem, the approach takes adjacent members Q_p(x) and Q_{p-1}(x) to compute high-degree Q_{p+s}(x) and low-degree Q_{p-t}(x), with Q_{p-1}(x) serving as a bridge. Three directional formulations (degree increase, degree decrease, end-to-middle) are derived.
AI.result: Explicit recurrence relations with two-degree step are provided for Hermite, Gegenbauer, and Legendre polynomials. A precision comparison between the standard and proposed recurrence relations is performed.
AI.conclusion: The approach successfully extends the standard recursive formula to arbitrary step sizes and multiple directions, with demonstrated applicability to classical orthogonal polynomial families.

title: Well-posedness and Stability Analysis of Suspension Bridge Models Coupled with Cattaneo Heat Conduction: The Role of Viscoelastic Memory
summary: This paper focuses on investigating a class of suspension bridge models coupled with the Cattaneo heat conduction law, with special attention paid to two distinct scenarios: systems with viscoelastic memory and those without. Operator semigroup theory is adopted as the core mathematical tool to conduct a comprehensive analysis of the models' dynamic behaviors. For the memoryless suspension bridge system ($δ= 0$), we first establish its well-posedness (i.e., the existence and uniqueness of solutions). Further stability analysis reveals that this system exhibits polynomial decay behavior, where the decay rate depends on the structural parameter relationship of the bridge deck: a decay rate of $t^{-1/2}$ is achieved when $\frac{ρ_1}κ = \frac{ρ_2}{b}$, and the rate slows down to $t^{-1/4}$ when $\frac{ρ_1}κ \neq \frac{ρ_2}{b}$. Moreover, exponential stability is proven to be unachievable under specific coefficient configurations (i.e., $χ_0 \neq 0$, or $χ_0 = 0$ and $χ_1 = 0$, with $χ_0, χ_1$ being parameter-dependent coefficients). For the counterpart system incorporating viscoelastic memory ($δ= 1$), the well-posedness of solutions is also verified, and more importantly, the system is shown to achieve exponential decay. This indicates that viscoelastic memory significantly enhances system stability and accelerates energy dissipation compared to the memoryless case. By systematically exploring the regulatory role of viscoelastic memory in the stability of suspension bridge systems under the framework of Cattaneo thermal conduction, this study enriches and extends the existing research on suspension bridge models with viscoelastic memory.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes suspension bridge models with Cattaneo heat conduction, comparing memoryless systems (polynomial decay: t^{-1/2} or t^{-1/4}) and viscoelastic memory systems (exponential decay), showing viscoelastic memory significantly enhances stability.
